summary: My full name is Ricky Lawrence Brinkley, but everyone calls me Rick Brinkley. I was born on July 25th, 1961, in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma. Throughout my life, I've had a diverse career including working in television in Tulsa, Baltimore, and New York City, and later becoming a pastor in Collinsville. I've also struggled with a gambling addiction that began in my early forties while working at the Better Business Bureau. Family and faith have always been central to who I am.

I was born in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, on July 25th, 1961. It's where my story begins before moving on to other places like Tulsa and Collinsville. Oklahoma City is the place of my birth and the starting point of my journey through life and career.

My mother was from Collinsville, Oklahoma, and graduated from there. I also went to school in Collinsville, completing my grades there before moving on to college. Later in life, I became the pastor of the Collinsville Community Church for several years, deeply connecting me to the community and its history.

My father, Donald Daniel Brinkley, was from Monticello, Arkansas. His early life was tough; his father died in a bus accident when my dad was eight, and he had to drop out of school to pick cotton and later drove a taxi cab at 13. Despite these hardships, he became a respected general manager of a car dealership in Tulsa and was a beloved figure in the community.
cues: car dealership general manager in Tulsa

summary: My father, Donald Daniel Brinkley, came from Monticello, Arkansas, and had a challenging childhood, losing his father in a bus accident at age eight. He left school early, worked picking cotton, and was driving a taxi by 13. Despite never graduating high school, he worked his way up to become the general manager of a car dealership in Tulsa and was known for his kindness and generosity to many people in the community.

I have fond memories of tailgating at the Oklahoma State Games, where my grandson Christian, nicknamed 'Pie,' and I would make cheesecakes together. Christian lives in Houston, and when he and his wife came up for the games, they requested specific cheesecakes for the tailgate. It was a special way to share my love of cooking and pass on family traditions during this event.

My grandson Christian lives in Houston with his wife. They came up to Oklahoma for the Oklahoma State Games, where we shared time cooking cheesecakes for the tailgate. Houston is where Christian has taken on responsibilities in his church, including cooking for large groups, showing how the family tradition of cooking continues there.

I met up with several people in Dallas, including my grandson Christian, where we shopped for spices he uses for smoking meats. Dallas was a place where we connected over cooking and shared family moments. It was part of the ongoing relationship I have with my grandson and his culinary interests.

During my junior year of college, I landed an internship at Channel Six in Tulsa, which turned into a job that I held for a couple of years. This was my first real step into the television industry and helped launch my career in broadcasting. It was a formative experience that led me to work in other cities like Baltimore and New York City.

After working in Tulsa, I moved to Baltimore to work for a TV station there. It was part of my early broadcasting career before moving on to New York City. While in Baltimore, I also worked with kids and began to feel a calling toward ministry, which influenced my later decisions.

I worked in New York City as one of the producers of the Sally Jessy Raphael show. This was a significant part of my television career before I decided to return to school and pursue seminary studies. New York City was a busy and formative time professionally, but it also set the stage for my transition into ministry.
cues: producer of Sally Jessy Raphael show

summary: I was a producer on The Sally Jessy Raphael Show while working in New York City. This role was a highlight of my television career and gave me valuable experience in production. It was during this time that I was still exploring my professional path before moving into ministry.

When I needed to finish my bachelor's degree, I attended the University Center at Tulsa, which was a consortium including OU, OSU, Langston, and NSU. I shopped around for the fastest degree I could get and ended up with a Liberal Education degree from Langston University through UCAT. This step was crucial for getting into Oral Roberts University's School of Theology later on.

The University of Oklahoma was one of the institutions involved in the University Center at Tulsa consortium where I completed my bachelor's degree. Although I did not attend OU directly, it was part of the educational network that helped me finish my degree. This was an important step in my academic and professional journey.

Oklahoma State University was part of the University Center at Tulsa consortium where I completed my bachelor's degree. Later, I also attended Oklahoma State University's School of Education briefly when I considered pursuing a doctorate in higher education administration. Although I started the doctorate, I did not finish due to job commitments.
cues: doctoral studies started but not completed

summary: Langston University was the school that granted me my bachelor's degree through the University Center at Tulsa. They gave me credit for my previous coursework and awarded me a Liberal Education degree, which was enough to get me into seminary. This degree was a key milestone in my educational path.

Northeastern State University, based in Tahlequah, was one of the institutions in the University Center at Tulsa consortium. While I did not attend NSU directly, it was part of the educational network available to me when I was finishing my degree. It represents part of the broader educational resources I accessed during that time.

After finishing my bachelor's degree, I enrolled in Oral Roberts University's School of Theology, where I earned my master's degree. This was a pivotal moment as I transitioned from television to ministry. Eventually, I became the pastor of the Collinsville Community Church, a role that grew out of my theological education at ORU.

I served as the pastor of the Collinsville Community Church for many years. This church was founded during the Depression by a group of women who couldn't afford a minister, which gave it a rich history that connected deeply with me. Being a pastor there meant more than preaching; I was involved in all aspects of community life and support.

The Collinsville Community Church where I was pastor is affiliated with the Assembly of God denomination. This connection shaped much of the church's theology and community outreach. My ministry was influenced by the beliefs and practices of the Assembly of God tradition.

I worked at the Better Business Bureau during the time when my gambling addiction began in my early forties. It was a period when I was juggling many responsibilities, including church work and community involvement. The addiction started when I went gambling with friends and quickly escalated, overshadowing everything else in my life.

I used to attend meetings twice a year with a group that met in Atlantic City and Las Vegas. At those times, gambling was a social activity, like playing blackjack with friends, and not an addiction. These trips were part of my life before my gambling problem escalated later on.

Las Vegas was one of the two cities where my group held meetings twice a year, alongside Atlantic City. During those trips, gambling was casual and social, mostly playing blackjack with friends. This was before my gambling addiction developed in my early forties.
